retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Bone -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: With a mechanism responsible for building up bone, osteoclasts are the large, multinucleated cells on the surface of bones in pits called Howship lacunae responsible for removing calcium from bone by secreting proteolytic enzymes and hydrogen ions to dissolve the calcium hydroxyapatite crystals. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Osteoblasts -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Bone is approximately 10% water, 30% organic, and 60% inorganic. The organic component is approximately 85 to 90% collagen (primarily type 1 -resisting tensile forces), proteoglycans (resisting compressive forces), non-collagenous proteins (osteocalcin and osteonectin) and glycoproteins (osteopontin). The inorganic component, or mineralized matrix, is composed of hydroxyapatite crystals [Ca10(PO4)6(OH)2] that provides protection and support while serving as the body's repository for calcium and phosphate. [2] [3] [4] [5] Osteoblasts also indirectly regulate osteoclast formation and bone remodeling by cell-cell contact, paracrine signaling, and cell-bone matrix interaction. [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Bone -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Once the osteoid is mineralized, the precursor cells get surrounded by organic intracellular substances called lacunae to become fully developed and matured into osteocytes. The mineral content is chiefly hydroxyapatite crystals composed of mainly calcium and phosphate ions but can occasionally have sodium and carbonite as well. [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Phosphate -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Endochondral Ossification : Phosphate is responsible for endochondral ossification of the bone as increased intracellular phosphate levels induce apoptosis of the terminally differentiated chondrocytes. Teeth : Phosphate is important for mineralization of all the structural components of the teeth i.e., it is an integral component of enamel, dentin, cementum, and alveolar bone. [11] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Osteoblasts -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Osteoblasts are colloquially referred to as cells that "build" bone. These cells are directly responsible for osteogenesis (or ossification). Osteoblasts synthesize and deposit organic bone matrix (osteoid) proteins that will mineralize in both developing skeletons and during the process of bone remodeling that occurs continuously throughout an individual's life. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Calcium -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium is an essential element that serves an important role in skeletal mineralization. More than 99% of the calcium in the body is stored in bone as hydroxyapatite. Calcium in this form provides skeletal strength as well as a reservoir for calcium to be released into the serum.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Bone -- Function -- Bone Extracellular Matrix
retrieved_docs.content: The bone extracellular matrix makes up 90% of the overall bone volume and consists of inorganic (mineral) and organic matrices. Inorganic bone matrix accounts for 99% of the body's storage of calcium, 85% of the phosphorus, and 40% to 60% of the magnesium and sodium. It is mainly in the form of hydroxyapatite [Ca10(PO4)6(OH)2] to provide the bone its strength, stiffness, and resistance to compressive forces. Organic bone matrix is secreted by osteoblasts and is predominantly type I collagen; this also contains glycoproteins, growth factors, and proteoglycans. Growth factors (such as osteocalcin, osteonectin, and bone sialoprotein) play important roles in osteoid formation, mineralization, and bone remodeling. The organic matrix gives bone its form and provides resistance to tensile forces. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Osteocytes -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Osteocytes also control osteoblastic and osteoclastic activities within a basic multicellular unit (BMU), a transient anatomic structure for insulating bone remodeling. Osteocytic proteins like sclerostin contribute to mineral metabolism aside from other molecules such as  MEPE, FGF-23, PHEX, and DMP-1, which help to regulate phosphate and biomineralization. Osteocytes also regulate bone mass and act as an essential endocrine regulator of phosphate metabolism. [4] [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Bone -- Function -- Bone Cells
retrieved_docs.content: Osteoblasts (bone-forming cells): They are tightly packed on the surface of the bone. They synthesize and secrete bone matrix (osteoid). They also regulate bone mineralization by secreting alkaline phosphatase (a marker for bone formation) and a set of proteins known as dentin matrix protein (DMP-1) and bone sialoprotein, which act as nucleators for mineralization. Osteocalcin and osteonectin are calcium and phosphate-binding proteins secreted by osteoblasts, which regulate the deposition of minerals by regulating the number of hydroxyapatite crystals. Osteoblasts ultimately have one of two fates: (1) remain quiescent osteoblasts lining cells or (2) become osteocytes. Osteoblasts regulate osteoclastogenesis (osteoclast formation) and osteocyte formation. Vitamin D and parathyroid hormone (PTH) stimulate osteoblasts to secrete macrophage cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) and express osteoprotegerin ligands, which are important for osteoclastogenesis (see Image. Osteoclastogenesis). [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Bones -- Structure and Function -- Bone Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Bone hardening requires mineralization, making bones the body's main calcium and phosphate reservoir. These ions are typically obtained from the diet, eg, dairy products. [3] However, calcium and phosphate can easily deplete if not stored in bones. Mineralization transforms the osteoblast into the mature bone cell, the osteocyte.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Bone Remodeling -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Once the fusion of osteoblast and osteoclastic precursors has occurred, the resulting multinucleated osteoclast attaches to the bone surface and commences resorption. These cells use a combination of lysosomal enzymes and hydrogen ions to break down the bone matrix. This bone matrix has an inorganic portion of calcium phosphate crystals (hydroxyapatite) and an organic portion comprised of collagen, proteoglycans, and glycoproteins. The resorption process leaves "scooped out" regions of the bone matrix (Howship lacunae). It is believed that mononuclear macrophage lineage cells then conduct a "reversal" phase, which continues to degrade and deposit organic material while releasing growth factors to initiate the bone deposition phase. [3] Osteoblasts

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Phosphate -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: X- linked hypophosphatemic rickets: it results from a mutation in the phosphate regulating endopeptidase homolog (PHEX gene)  that leads to unregulated FGF23 production. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Hydroxyapatite from natural sources is commonly fabricated from materials such as fishbone, coral, bovine bone, eggshell, and seashells through a calcination process. Hydroxyapatite produced from these natural sources is often nonstoichiometric due to the presence of trace ions. [13] [17] These traces of ions, which include cations such as Na + , K + , Mg 2+ , Sr 2+ , Zn 2+ , and Al 3+ , as well as anions such as F - , Cl - , SO 4 2- , and CO 3 2- , are beneficial for promoting rapid bone regeneration. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Phosphate -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Bone mineralization : phosphate is responsible for mineralization of the bony matrix. This process begins in the matrix vesicle, which are extracellular structures derived from the cell membrane of the osteoblast and chondrocytes. Matrix vesicles acquire phosphate by two pathways: Tissue nonspecific Alkaline phosphatase present within matrix vesicles hydrolyzes phosphoric esters to inorganic phosphate. Matrix vesicles uptake extracellular phosphate via Type II Na/Pi cotransporter. Matrix vesicles form hydroxyapatite crystals from calcium and phosphate; these crystals mineralize the extracellular matrix of the bone.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Bone Ossification -- Cellular -- Osteoclasts
retrieved_docs.content: Osteoclasts are multinucleated cells that function in bone resorption. [6] [7] They are derived from macrophages and enter the bone through blood vessels. Each osteoclast has numerous processes that extend into the matrix and secrete hydrogen ions, causing acidification and break down of bone. Osteoclast function is under tight control; overactivity results in osteoporosis, while decreased activity results in osteopetrosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Enchondroma -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Bone is a unique connective tissue type, being one that undergoes mineralization. Bone's inorganic component consists of calcium hydroxyapatite, which imparts strength and toughness. This mineral is also the body's main calcium (99%) and phosphate (85%) reservoir and stores 65% of its sodium and magnesium. Meanwhile, bone's organic component is comprised of bone cells and matrix proteins. The bone cells include the following:

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium acetate and tricalcium phosphate are both classified as calcium compounds, indicating that each belongs to the broader category of calcium‑containing substances. As members of this class, they share the fundamental chemical characteristic of providing bioavailable calcium and interacting with phosphate metabolism. Recognizing calcium acetate and tricalcium phosphate as calcium compounds helps clarify their roles in medical nutrition, calcium supplementation, and the management of bone health and phosphate balance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1306
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium Compounds represent a class of calcium‑containing chemical substances that include several distinct calcium salts. Among these are calcium glycerophosphate, which is a calcium‑based glycerophosphate compound, calcium acetate, a calcium salt of acetic acid, and tricalcium phosphate, a calcium phosphate used in various medical and nutritional contexts. All three entities share calcium as a central component, linking them together as members of the broader category of calcium compounds that are relevant for applications such as phosphate binding, calcium supplementation, and bone health management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Kalcitriol and dihydrotachysterol are both classified as members of the “Vitamin D and analogues” group, indicating that they are vitamin D–related compounds that share similar biological activity in regulating calcium and phosphate metabolism. This classification highlights their common therapeutic role as vitamin D analogues used to treat conditions such as hypocalcemia, secondary hyperparathyroidism, and certain bone disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 148
retrieved_docs.content: activity, phosphate chelation, inhibition of bone resorption) and their synergistic inclusion with iron, calcium, other vitamins and trace minerals underpin their therapeutic, preventive and formulation‑stabilizing roles in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1916
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium acetate and calcium phosphate dibasic are both classified as calcium compounds. As members of the broader calcium compounds category, they share the fundamental property of containing calcium ions, yet they are applied differently in medical practice. Calcium acetate is frequently used as a phosphate binder for patients with chronic kidney disease, helping to control serum phosphate levels, while calcium phosphate dibasic serves as a calcium source in nutritional supplements and supports bone health. Recognizing that both agents belong to the calcium compounds group aids clinicians in understanding their chemical characteristics and therapeutic roles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 362
retrieved_docs.content: Other bone disease and musculoskeletal deformities are associated with bone resorption, indicating that these conditions often involve increased breakdown of bone tissue, which contributes to skeletal weakening and structural abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 565
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium Oyster Shell is specifically linked to vitamin D, indicating a narrower, synergistic relationship between the calcium source and the vitamin that supports calcium absorption. IRON/MULTIVITAMINS LIQUID includes vitamin D as an ingredient, and multivitamin tablets also contain vitamin D, while vitamin D may be added as an additive in vitamin IV solutions. Vitamin D itself functions as an inhibitor of bone resorption (inhibitory kostní resorpce), a key mechanism that helps maintain bone density. Because of this role, vitamin D is directly related to osteoporosis (هشاشة العظام), highlighting its importance in the prevention and management of bone‑weakening conditions. Together, these formulations and the physiological actions of vitamin D underscore its central place in supporting skeletal health and addressing disorders such as osteoporosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1895
retrieved_docs.content: Mineral supplements are classified both as calcium‑protein preparations and as molybdenum‑related supplements, indicating that they encompass formulations that combine calcium with protein components while also providing the essential trace element molybdenum. This dual categorization reflects the broader role of mineral supplements in delivering multiple essential nutrients to support metabolic and physiological functions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 455
retrieved_docs.content: Drugs affecting bone structure and mineralization are classified as bone morphogenetic proteins, known in Czech as kostní morfogenetické proteiny. These proteins are key signaling molecules that regulate the formation, remodeling, and mineralization of bone tissue, and therapeutic agents that target or mimic them can influence skeletal health and disease management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1877
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium is categorized as a mineral, indicating that it belongs to the broader group of minerály and is recognized as an essential trace element within this class.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 641
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium supplements (مكملات الكالسيوم) are intended for therapeutic use, meaning they are administered to treat or prevent medical conditions related to calcium deficiency or bone health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2030
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin D and analogues is a pharmacological class that encompasses both natural vitamin D2 (ergocalciferol) and synthetic compounds such as dihydrotachysterol; dihydrotachysterol is identified as a member of the Vitamin D and analogues group, and vitamin D2 is likewise included within this broader category. This classification highlights the relationship between the natural form of vitamin D2 and its analogues, informing clinicians about the spectrum of agents that share similar biological activity and can be considered when selecting vitamin D–related therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2450
retrieved_docs.content: Calcitonin is biologically related to parathormone, the principal parathyroid hormone. Parathormone is classified within the broader group of parathyroid hormones and analogues, a therapeutic class that also encompasses the synthetic analogue teriparatide. Understanding the connections among calcitonin, parathormone, and their related analogues aids clinicians in comprehending calcium‑regulating pathways and in selecting appropriate hormone‑based treatments for disorders of bone metabolism.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 746
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium Oyster Shell is linked to vitamin D through a specific, narrower relationship that suggests it may serve as a complementary source or enhancer of vitamin D activity. IRON/MULTIVITAMINS LIQUID lists vitamin D as an ingredient, and multivitamin tablets also contain vitamin D as a key component. The concept of inhibitory kostní resorpce is identified as a form of vitamin D activity that helps suppress bone resorption. Vitamin D is further noted to be related to هشاشة العظام (osteoporosis), underscoring its important role in maintaining bone health and preventing excessive bone loss. Together, these connections highlight vitamin D as a central factor in bone metabolism, with various supplements such as calcium oyster shell products, iron/multivitamin liquids, and multivitamin tablets providing it to support bone strength and reduce the risk of osteoporosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2353
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium protein preparations and calcium lactate are both classified as mineral supplements, representing specific types of calcium‑containing mineral supplement products used to support nutritional needs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 452
retrieved_docs.content: hydrolyzáty may be described by their ultrastructure. Prostaglandin antagonists are linked mechanistically to cyclooxygenase inhibition. Therapeutically, the ReFit bone substitute is mapped to both hyaluronic acid and collagen, indicating its composition of these biomaterials for bone regeneration, and a Selenium supplement is associated with selenium as a source of this essential trace element. This network of entities, hierarchical classifications, and qualifiers supports comprehensive patient assessment, biomarker‑driven diagnostics and targeted therapeutic interventions.
